howItWorks.intro: This calories burned calculator uses standard formulas to provide accurate results.
howItWorks.outro: Individual results vary based on metabolism, genetics, and activity level. Consult your doctor before starting any program.

faq.question: What is MET?
faq.answer: Metabolic Equivalent of Task. MET 1 = sitting still. Walking = 3-4 MET, cycling = 6-8, running = 8-12, swimming = 6-10. Higher MET = more intense.

faq.question: How accurate is this?
faq.answer: MET-based estimates are within 15-20% for most people. Actual burn depends on intensity, fitness level, body composition, and environmental factors.

faq.question: How many calories to lose 1 pound?
faq.answer: Roughly 3,500 calories equals 1 pound of fat. A 500 cal/day deficit through diet and exercise produces about 1 lb/week loss.

faq.question: Does exercise after-burn count?
faq.answer: Yes — EPOC (excess post-exercise oxygen consumption) can burn 6-15% additional calories after intense exercise. This calculator estimates direct burn only.

faq.question: Is the Calories Burned Calculator free to use?
faq.answer: Yes, completely free with no signup required. Use it as many times as you need — there are no limits or hidden fees.

sections.title: What Is the Calories Burned Calculator?
sections.body: The Calories Burned Calculator is a free online tool designed for athletes and fitness enthusiasts who need quick, accurate calculations in the fitness and training space. By entering your weight, activity met, duration, you get instant results including calories burned, calories per hour, fat equivalent. No formulas to memorize, no spreadsheets to build — just enter your numbers and get the answer in seconds. Whether you're a beginner or experienced professional, this calculator saves you time and eliminates guesswork.

sections.title: Why This Calculation Matters
sections.body: Getting calories burned right can make the difference between success and costly mistakes. In fitness and training, small errors compound quickly. Manual calculations are error-prone and time-consuming, especially under pressure. This calculator applies proven formulas used by athletes and fitness enthusiasts worldwide, giving you confidence that your numbers are correct. Use it to optimize your training with precision and avoid common pitfalls that trip up beginners.

sections.title: When Should You Use This Calculator?
sections.body: This tool is most useful when you know your weight and need to find the right calories burned. It's also great for quick estimates before committing to a decision, and to double-check manual calculations or professional quotes, and when comparing different scenarios side by side. Bookmark this page and come back whenever you need a fast, reliable answer — the calculator is always free and requires no signup.

howToSteps.title: Enter Your Weight (lbs)
howToSteps.description: Type or select your weight in the field provided. Use the most accurate value available for best results.

howToSteps.title: Enter Your Activity MET (3=Walk 6=Cycle 8=Run 10=Swim)
howToSteps.description: Type or select your activity met in the field provided. Use the most accurate value available for best results.

howToSteps.title: Enter Your Duration (minutes)
howToSteps.description: Type or select your duration in the field provided. Use the most accurate value available for best results.

howToSteps.title: Click Calculate
howToSteps.description: Hit the Calculate button to run the numbers. Results appear instantly below.

howToSteps.title: Review Your Results
howToSteps.description: Check your calories burned, calories per hour, fat equivalent. Use these figures to inform your next decision or compare against alternative scenarios.

tips: Double-check your weight before calculating — even small input errors can significantly change your results.
Run the calculator with different values to compare scenarios and find the optimal approach for your situation.
Pay attention to both calories burned and calories per hour — they work together to give you the full picture.
Bookmark this page for quick access next time you need to optimize your training.
If you're unsure about your duration, start with a conservative estimate and adjust from there.
